content:
3 min read 1 hour ago -- 1. Introduction In the previous article, we learned how to use Route Parameters and Query Parameters to pass data between components. In this article, we’ll learn about Child Routes, which allow us to create nested navigation inside a parent component. 2. What are Child Routes? A Child Route is a route that exists inside another route (called the parent route). Instead of navigating to completely different pages, Child Routes display different components inside a specific section of the parent component. This helps us organize related pages together. 3. What We Will Build Press enter or click to view image in full size We’ll create a simple Dashboard page with three child pages: • Dashboard • Profile • Settings • Reports When the user clicks these links, only the content inside the Dashboard changes. 4. Create Components ng generate component home ng generate component about ng generate component contact ng generate component dashboard ng generate component profile ng generate component settings ng generate component reports5. Configure Child Routes app.routes.ts import { Routes } from '@angular/router'; import { HomeComponent } from './home/home.component'; import { DashboardComponent } from './dashboard/dashboard.component'; import { ProfileComponent } from './profile/profile.component'; import { SettingsComponent } from './settings/settings.component'; import { ReportsComponent } from './reports/reports.component'; import { AboutComponent } from './about/about.component'; import { ContactComponent } from './contact/contact.component';export const routes: Routes = [ { path: '', component: HomeComponent }, { path: 'about', component: AboutComponent }, { path: 'contact', component: ContactComponent }, { path: 'dashboard', component: DashboardComponent, children: [ { path: 'profile', component: ProfileComponent }, { path: 'settings', component: SettingsComponent }, { path: 'reports', component: ReportsComponent } ] } ]; 6. Understanding the Route Configuration The dashboard route is the parent route. The routes inside the children array are called child routes. Whenever the URL starts with dashboard, Angular first loads the DashboardComponent. Then, depending on the remaining URL, it loads the appropriate child component. 7. Add Navigation And in app.component.html:
Home About Contact Dashboard
And in app.component.ts: import { Component } from '@angular/core'; import { RouterLink, RouterOutlet } from '@angular/router';@Component({ selector: 'app-root', standalone: true, imports: [RouterOutlet,RouterLink], templateUrl: './app.component.html', styleUrl: './app.component.css' }) export class AppComponent { title = 'child-routes-demo'; } 7. Add Navigation Inside Dashboard dashboard.component.html

Dashboard

Profile Settings Reports
8. Why do we use another router-outlet? The first router-outlet in AppComponent displays the parent component. The second router-outlet inside DashboardComponent displays the child components. Without this second router-outlet, Angular would know which child route to load, but it would have no place to display it. 9. Add Router Imports dashboard.component.ts import { Component } from '@angular/core'; import { RouterLink, RouterOutlet } from '@angular/router';@Component({ selector: 'app-dashboard', standalone: true, imports: [RouterLink, RouterOutlet], templateUrl: './dashboard.component.html', styleUrl: './dashboard.component.css' }) export class DashboardComponent { } 10. Child Components profile.component.html

Profile Page

settings.component.html

Settings Page

reports.component.html

Reports Page

11. URLs When you open these URLs: /dashboard/profileAngular displays: DashboardProfile Page /dashboard/settingsAngular displays: DashboardSettings Page /dashboard/reportsAngular displays: DashboardReports Page Notice that the Dashboard stays the same, while only the child content changes. 12. Add CSS (Optional) dashboard.component.css , app.component.css nav{ display:flex; gap:20px; margin-bottom:20px; }nav a{ text-decoration:none; padding:8px 15px; background:#3498db; color:white; border-radius:5px; } nav a:hover{ background:#2980b9; } 13. How Child Routing Works User Opens /dashboard/profile │ ▼ Angular Finds Parent Route │ ▼ DashboardComponent Loads │ ▼ Dashboard router-outlet Found │ ▼ ProfileComponent Loads │ ▼ Display Dashboard + Profile16. Conclusion In this article, we learned: • What Child Routes are. • How to configure child routes using the children property. • Why a second router-outlet is required. • How Angular displays child components inside a parent component. In the next article, we’ll learn about Route Guards (CanActivate) and how to protect routes from unauthorized users.
